text:
The intestinal microbiota has been shown to affect the communication along the gut-brain axis. Here we investigated the role of the microbiota on brain cerebral metabolism comparing germ-free (GF) mice with ex-germ-free (Ex-GF) mice, being Ex-GF mice mice raised in GF conditions and then microbiotically colonized by fecal flora from specific pathogen-free (SPF) mice.

Previous studies have identified various cognitive impairments in genetically deficient (GF) mice when compared to their conventionally housed, otherwise genetically identical, controls (Ex-GF). Here we used capillary electrophoresis time-of-flight mass spectrometry (CE-TOFMS) to investigate the cerebral metabolome of GF and Ex-GF mice. We were able to detect 196 features that were present across all 3 replicates of each group. Comparing GF to Ex-GF mice, we detected 38 features (metabolites) that differed in concentration between the two groups at a significance threshold of p < 0.05. Of these, approximately 10 have been identified in previous literature and characterized as significant contributors to normal brain function, while the role of the remaining 28 is unknown.

Our study demonstrates for the first time a connection between intestinal microbiota and brain glucose metabolism. We identified a significant gut microbiota effect on brain biochemistry through the microbiota-gut-brain axis. The gut microbiome is therefore deeply involved in brain function and this study opens new avenues in the research of brain development, behavior, memory and of various neurological diseases.
explanation: The passage reads like a concise, abstract-like report with plausible experimental specifics, but it contains terminological inconsistencies and a stray 'mice mice' typo that are characteristic of AI-generated drafts or automated text generation.
